subject: Statistics
title: Intention to Treat Analysis
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Intention to Treat Analysis.

hook.text: Half the patients in a drug trial stop taking the drug. Should you analyze only those who finished?

buildup.text: Intention-to-treat analysis includes everyone randomized, even those who dropped out or switched groups.

discovery.text: This preserves the randomization that makes the trial valid. Dropping people reintroduces selection bias.

twist.text: It makes the drug look weaker — some analyzed patients didn't even take it. But it reflects real-world usage.

climax.text: Real patients miss doses, switch drugs, and drop out. Intention to treat gives the most honest estimate.

punchline.text: Analyze everyone you randomized. Even the ones who quit.

quiz.question: Why does intention-to-treat analysis include patients who stopped taking the drug?
options: Excluding them would break the randomization and reintroduce bias
It makes the drug look more effective
Regulators require a minimum sample size
